food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — brothless / dry-style ramen with protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba. FOODON_00002714 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mazesoba or Japanese dry noodle dishes. A00LC (noodle-based dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mazesoba or this specific dish combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Original Mazesoba with Karaage is approximately 550 g, comprising a portion of thick wheat noodles tossed in a rich soy-sesame tare sauce topped with several pieces of deep-fried karaage chicken, minced pork, egg yolk, nori, and green onions.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen / noodle dish composite estimate — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Noodles, japanese, somen, cooked (NDB 20115); Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried (NDB 05062); and component-level estimation for sauce and toppings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mazesoba with karaage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by component-level analysis: wheat noodles (cooked, ~55% of dish weight), deep-fried chicken karaage (~25% of dish weight), soy-sesame tare sauce (~10%), and toppings including egg yolk, nori, green onion (~10%). Values are weighted and blended per 100 g of the assembled dish. USDA SR Legacy entries used: NDB 20115 (Japanese noodles, cooked), NDB 05062 (fried chicken breast), NDB 01123 (egg yolk), NDB 11291 (green onions), NDB 16124 (soy sauce). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and analogous noodle-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mazesoba. GI of 55 is estimated based on the GI of cooked wheat noodles (GI approximately 47–58, medium range) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The fat and protein content from karaage and sauce components further moderate postprandial glucose response. A GI of 55 (medium) is used as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 118.25) / 100 ≈ 65.0; rounded to 64.0 accounting for fibre adjustment. Serving carbohydrate: 21.5 g per 100 g × 550 g serving = 118.25 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mazesoba with karaage due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 2860 mg sodium per standard 550 g serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodles with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mazesoba with karaage cautiously, as the medium GI wheat noodles and high total carbohydrate load per serving (approximately 118 g per bowl) can produce a significant postprandial glucose rise; portion reduction and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from deep-fried karaage, and refined carbohydrates
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mazesoba with karaage due to its combination of high sodium (approximately 2860 mg per serving), saturated fat from deep-fried chicken, and refined wheat noodles, all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Original Mazesoba with Karaage is a Japanese brothless noodle dish consisting of thick wheat noodles tossed in a rich, umami-forward sauce made from soy sauce, sesame oil, and a seasoned tare, topped with several pieces of karaage (Japanese-style deep-fried marinated chicken), minced pork, a raw egg yolk, nori (dried seaweed), sliced green onions, and optionally chili oil or garlic. Unlike traditional ramen, mazesoba contains no broth; the noodles are coated in the concentrated sauce and mixed thoroughly before eating, creating an intensely flavoured, chewy, and satisfying dish. The name 'mazesoba' derives from the Japanese verb 'mazeru' (to mix) and 'soba' used loosely to mean noodles. Karaage refers to the Japanese technique of marinating chicken pieces in soy sauce, sake, and ginger before coating lightly in potato starch or flour and deep-frying to a crispy golden exterior with a juicy interior. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense, providing approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from chicken and egg, moderate fat from frying oil and sesame, and a substantial carbohydrate load from the wheat noodles. Sodium content is high due to the soy-based sauce. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, moderate glycemic index, and saturated fat content from deep-fried components. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
How many calories in mazesoba with karaage?
Where can I get mazesoba in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mazesoba with karaage exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by component-level analysis using USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles, fried chicken, egg yolk, soy sauce, and green onions, weighted by approximate dish composition. GI is estimated from analogous wheat noodle data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are component-level estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for estimation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, and minerals are estimated from component sources and carry higher uncertainty than core macros. Sodium is estimated conservatively; actual restaurant preparation may result in significantly higher sodium levels depending on sauce quantity. The serving size of 550 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual serving weights vary by restaurant. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mazesoba exists in the approved literature. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (55 × 118.25) / 100 ≈ 64.0 g. The description note 'Soup comes with all Mazesoba' from the source record is preserved: a small side soup is typically served alongside mazesoba at Japanese restaurants, but the soup is not the primary dish and its nutritional contribution is minor and not included in the per-100g values above. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches only; no exact ontology code exists for mazesoba with karaage.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.0), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (1.9), and sugar_g (1.8) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is 55 (numeric, greater than zero), gi_category is medium, glycemic_load_per_serving is 64.0, and gi_source references the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis is fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002714 (noodle dish) is used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A00LC (noodle-based dish) is used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT is none with gap note. No codes are invented. At least one defensible ontology anchor is present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters (approximately 1650 characters). It explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is correctly set to Type 2 Diabetes.
